summary: Teaches -er/-ir verb conjugation with key vowel /e/, introduces 'vender' (to sell), feminine endings (-ión, -idad, -a), the -ity to -idad conversion rule, double pronoun ordering (personal first), and 'nos' (us).

teachings.summary: For -er/-ir verbs (a closed historic group), the key vowel is /e/. He/she/it/you(formal) gets -e, they/you(plural) gets -en, you(informal) gets -es. The 'I' form is still -o for all verb types.
teachings.rule: -er/-ir verbs: -o (I), -e (he/she/it/you formal), -en (they/you plural), -es (you informal). Key vowel = e.

examples.english: to contaminate → it contaminates (-ar key vowel a)

teachings.notes: Although there are three endings (-ar, -er, -ir), there are only two conjugation groups: -ar verbs and -er/-ir verbs.

teachings.summary: Words ending in -a are usually feminine (la casa). Words ending in -ión are always feminine (la opinión). Words ending in -idad are always feminine (la diversidad). Masculine words typically end in -o.
teachings.rule: Feminine endings: -a, -ión, -idad (all use 'la'). The gender belongs to the word, not the thing.

examples.english: the person (feminine word even for males)

teachings.notes: A man is 'una persona' — the word is feminine even when referring to a male.

teachings.summary: English words ending -ity become -idad in Spanish. All -idad words are feminine.
teachings.notes: From -idad words, you can guess adjectives by removing -idad and adding -o/-a: claridad → claro, generosidad → generoso.

teachings.summary: Adjectives ending in -o change to -a for feminine, and add -s for plural. -os (masculine plural), -as (feminine plural).
When two object pronouns appear together (e.g., 'me' and 'lo'), the personal one (me, te, nos) comes first.
teachings.rule: Personal pronoun (me, te, nos) comes before thing pronoun (lo, la, los, las).

examples.english: They don't sell it to me
Why don't they sell it to me?
Why don't they sell it to us?

teachings.notes: Think about the verb first, then add the pronouns. Don't try to translate the whole package at once.

teachings.summary: Nos means 'us'. It follows the same placement rules as me, te, lo.
Claro means 'clear' as an adjective, but is also used conversationally like 'of course' to show agreement or understanding.
teachings.notes: Derived from claridad. Used a lot in conversation: 'claro, claro, claro'.
